A History of Imperial Transfer
1898
When One Empire Died and Another Began
"School Begins" — Louis Dalrymple, Puck Magazine, January 25, 1899  ·  Public Domain, Library of Congress
Mariana Hernandez
"The most durable empires are the ones that insist they are not empires."

Preface

The Year Everyone Thinks They Know

"I am two and I am many."— José Martí, Versos sencillos, 1891

Start with what you were taught.

A decrepit Spanish empire, hollowed by misrule, clung to its last possessions in the Caribbean and the Pacific. Cuba was suffering. A battleship exploded in Havana harbor. The American press howled. A short, lopsided war followed — four months, over before summer ended. Cuba was liberated. Puerto Rico was acquired. The Philippines changed hands. Secretary of State John Hay called it a splendid little war. The textbooks agreed. Most still do.

That story is not a lie. It is something more dangerous than a lie. It is a true account of the surface of events arranged to prevent you from seeing what was underneath them.

Underneath: the Cuban revolutionary army had been fighting Spain to a standstill for three years before a single American soldier arrived. Underneath: the Filipino independence movement had already declared a republic and seized control of most of the archipelago. Underneath: Puerto Rico had just won genuine autonomy from Spain. Underneath: Hawaii's queen had been deposed by American sugar planters backed by American marines, and had been pleading for restoration ever since.

The war of 1898 did not create any of this. It inherited it, formalized it, and pointed it in a new direction.

—   —   —

The central claim of this book is simple: 1898 was not a rescue. It was a transfer. One empire weakened. Another moved into the space that weakness created — deliberately, with strategic preparation that had been underway for years, and with a moral vocabulary already in place to make the whole operation legible as something other than what it was.

Liberation. Civilization. Tutelage. Order. These were not cynical words deployed by men who knew they were lying. They were used with the conviction of people raised inside a worldview that made them true. The something else was always education. Always rescue. Always the necessary protection of people too vulnerable, too inexperienced, too unformed to protect themselves.

The people being protected had other words for it. They paid with their lives for the right to use them. Most of those words were never translated. Most of those lives do not appear in the standard accounts.

They appear in this one.

Part One

The Illusion of Endings

Empires do not end like fires go out — suddenly, with a last gasp of heat and then nothing. They decay. They hollow. They persist in appearance long after their substance has departed.

Chapter One

The Classroom of Empire

"We cannot fly from our world-duties; it is ours to execute the purpose of a fate that has driven us to be greater than our small intentions."— Senator Albert Beveridge, January 9, 1900
"School Begins" — Louis Dalrymple, Puck Magazine, January 25, 1899 Uncle Sam instructs Cuba, Porto Rico, Hawaii, and the Philippines. The blackboard reads: "The consent of the governed is a good thing in theory, but very rare in fact." Public Domain · Library of Congress · Reproduction No. LC-DIG-ppmsca-28668

Look at the picture. It appeared in Puck magazine in January 1899, drawn by Louis Dalrymple. A schoolroom. Uncle Sam at the blackboard, pointer in hand. In the front desks: small dark figures labeled Cuba, Puerto Rico, Hawaii, the Philippines — wide-eyed, the visual grammar of not-yet-knowing. In the back of the room, older students sit reading quietly. They are labeled: Texas. California. New Mexico.

The cartoon's title is School Begins. Study it for a moment, because it is not a cartoon. It is a theory of history. It tells you the relationship between teacher and student is natural, inevitable, good. It tells you nothing about how the student arrived, who decided the terms of graduation, or what happens to a student who refuses to learn.

That is the schoolroom. Not a building. A way of seeing. Assumptions so embedded in the thinking of 1898 that they did not need to be argued — they could be drawn.

The Architecture of Civilizational Thinking

Three ideas held the structure together, each reinforcing the others until the whole became nearly airtight. First: civilization is a ladder, and not everyone is on the same rung. Second: the more advanced have a duty — not a right — to assist the less advanced. Rule is service. Third: the colonized are not yet ready. When will they be? When the colonizer decides.

Together, these produced liberal imperialism: the genuine conviction, held by serious men of real intelligence, that governing people without their consent was a moral act. You cannot argue a man out of a position he did not arrive at through argument.

The schoolroom ideology had one extraordinary feature: it converted evidence of resistance into evidence of need. If a Filipino leader argued his people were capable of self-government, this could be absorbed without difficulty. Of course they believed they were capable. Children often overestimated their readiness. The desire for independence proved the immaturity.

Chapter Two

Empires Do Not Die All at Once

"Spain once possessed an empire on which the sun never set. Now the sun never rises on what remains."— Cuban newspaper editorial, 1896

In the summer of 1897, a traveler arriving in Havana would have found a city caught between two realities. On one level, the machinery of Spanish empire was still functioning. On another, it had already ceased to function in the ways that mattered most. The countryside was in open rebellion. The colonial treasury was bankrupt. The metropolitan government in Madrid was internally divided and unable to formulate a coherent response.

Spain was losing legitimacy faster than it was losing territory. Its authority had collapsed long before its military forces were actually defeated. The empire was a shell. The question was not whether it would end, but how.

The United States was not defeating a rival power. It was claiming the inheritance of a collapsing empire before that inheritance could fall to those who had actually been fighting for it.

Part Two

The Crisis Before the War

The war of 1898 did not create the crisis. Cuba had been at war with Spain for thirty years. The United States did not enter a stable situation and destabilize it. It entered an unstable situation and took advantage of it.

Chapter Three

Cuba Before Intervention

"Cuba must be free — from Spain and from every other power."— Revolutionary proclamation, La Mejorana, 1895

Before the Maine. Before the headlines. Before Roosevelt and his cavalry — Cuba was already at war. A war running since 1868, when Carlos Manuel de Céspedes walked to the steps of his estate, freed the people he had enslaved, and told them to take up arms. The cry he raised — the Grito de Yara — began the Ten Years' War, the first sustained Cuban war of independence. It is not remembered at all in most American accounts of 1898, which is itself a fact worth sitting with.

By the 1890s, Cuba was not a society waiting for liberation. It was a society that had been building its own liberation for thirty years. The revolution's ideology, shaped most decisively by José Martí, was built on a radical claim: Cuban nationhood was inseparable from racial equality — una nación sin razas, a nation without races. The most gifted military commander the revolution produced — Antonio Maceo, called the Bronze Titan — was Black.

The Women the Revolution Made

The history of the Cuban independence movement has always been easier to tell as a story of generals. But the revolution was not made only by soldiers and poets. Women organized the asociaciones patrióticas — revolutionary clubs that were the financial and logistical backbone of the movement. Inside Cuba, women served as mambisas — spies passing intelligence through domestic networks that Spanish military intelligence consistently underestimated. The invisibility that colonial society imposed on women became a tactical advantage.

Chapter Four

Martí and the Fear of Replacement

"I have lived in the monster and I know its entrails."— José Martí, letter written the day before his death, May 18, 1895
José Martí, Key West, 1891 Photographed during his years organizing Cuban exile communities in the United States. He returned to Cuba to fight in 1895 and was killed within two weeks. Public Domain · c. 1891

José Martí died on May 19, 1895, two weeks after he had finally returned to Cuba to fight in the war he had spent fifteen years organizing from exile. He was killed at Dos Ríos — a small skirmish in the eastern province of Oriente — when, by some accounts, he rode directly toward the Spanish lines. He was forty-two years old.

His unfinished letter — written to his friend Manuel Mercado the day before his death — contains what may be the most prophetic sentence any Latin American political thinker has ever written: that he was in danger of giving his life to prevent, by the independence of Cuba, the United States from spreading over the Antilles and falling upon the lands of America.

He died before he could finish the letter. His fear proved entirely justified, with a speed that would have astonished even him.

"The only protection against replacement was to be so thoroughly independent that there was nothing left to replace."

— José Martí, 1892

Martí's analysis of the United States was not born of hostility. He had lived in New York for fifteen years, admired much about American democracy, and written beautifully about the country. What he saw in those entrails was not simply malevolence but something structural: a pattern of expansion that followed economic interest dressed in democratic language, that celebrated freedom in the abstract while failing to respect it in the particular.

His response was the concept of pre-emptive sovereignty. Cuba had to achieve genuine independence before the United States had an opportunity to intervene. Once intervention came, it would be too late. The only protection against replacement was to be so thoroughly independent that there was nothing left to replace.

Chapter Five

Repression, Reconcentration, and Moral Theater

"Our inhumanity is not real; it is Spain's. We are only the mirror."— Attributed to William Randolph Hearst, disputed
Survivors of General Weyler's reconcentrado camps, Cuba, c. 1896–98 Forced relocation of the rural population into fortified camps killed an estimated 200,000–300,000 Cubans through disease and starvation. These photographs circulated widely in the American press, framing Cubans as passive victims in need of rescue rather than as agents of their own liberation. Public Domain · Published in the American press, c. 1897–1898

The reconcentrado camps were real. The bodies were real. The starvation and the disease and the systematic brutality of Spanish counter-insurgency were real. What American newspapers did was select and amplify and frame: they wrapped it in narrative — brave rebels, helpless civilians, Spanish monsters, American protectors — that organized raw events into a morally legible story.

Cuban suffering in the American press was primarily useful as an argument for American intervention. The Cubans appeared as passive victims rather than as agents of their own liberation. The Cuban revolutionary army, which by 1897 had been fighting Spain to a standstill for three years, was largely invisible. Suffering calls for rescue — not the kind of solidarity that would support an existing liberation movement and let it finish what it started.

The Reconcentrado Policy · Cuba, 1896–1898
Cuban civilian population forcibly relocated~300,000+
Deaths from camps (estimated)200,000 – 300,000
Equivalent U.S. death toll today~40 million
Same policy later applied by U.S. inPhilippines, 1900–1902
Part Three

The War That Made the Transfer Official

The war lasted approximately four months. What it decided was not the principle of Cuban independence — that had already been decided, by Cubans, over three years of revolutionary struggle — but the question of who would control the terms of whatever came next.

Chapter Six

Remember the Maine, Remember the Script

"The warship Maine was split in two by an enemy's secret infernal machine."— New York Journal headline, February 17, 1898
USS Maine entering Havana Harbor, January 25, 1898 The ship exploded three weeks later on February 15, killing 266 of 354 men aboard. Two Cubans sit on rocks in the foreground — unaware of what they are witnessing. The 1976 U.S. Navy investigation concluded the most likely cause was an internal coal bunker fire, not a Spanish mine. U.S. Naval Historical Center · Public Domain · NHHC NH 46774

On the night of February 15, 1898, the USS Maine was destroyed by an explosion in Havana harbor. The cause has never been definitively established. The first American naval investigation concluded an external mine was responsible — a conclusion that served the political purpose of blaming Spain, but was methodologically shoddy and almost certainly wrong.

None of this mattered in February and March of 1898. What mattered was the story, and the story required that Spain had killed American sailors. McKinley's final ultimatum to Spain was framed as a humanitarian demand. Spain agreed to virtually everything. The reconcentration policy was ended. The armistice was declared. McKinley went to Congress for a declaration of war anyway.

The point was not to get Spain to reform. The point was to establish American authority over what happened next. And that required the war.

Chapter Seven

A Short War, A Long Ambition

"It has been a splendid little war, begun with the highest motives..."— Secretary of State John Hay, letter to Theodore Roosevelt, July 27, 1898

The Spanish-American War lasted 113 days. Theodore Roosevelt's charge up Kettle Hill — usually misremembered as San Juan Hill — is the most famous image. The charge was accompanied by, and arguably less significant than, the advance of the Ninth and Tenth Cavalry — the Buffalo Soldiers — whose contribution was systematically minimized in subsequent accounts, including Roosevelt's own.

The most revealing episode of the war was not in Cuba at all. It was in Manila Bay, on May 1, 1898, where Commodore Dewey's Asiatic Squadron destroyed the Spanish fleet in seven hours, resulting in precisely zero American deaths. Dewey's orders had been prepared by Assistant Secretary of the Navy Theodore Roosevelt months before the war began, without the approval of his superiors. Aguinaldo understood the American offer as a commitment to Philippine independence. The Americans understood it as a tactical arrangement with no binding obligations. This discrepancy would have enormous consequences.

McKinley did not wake up one morning and discover he had acquired an archipelago of seven thousand islands. The Philippines was taken deliberately, by people who knew what they were doing and why.

Chapter Eight

The Treaty and the Paperwork of Empire

"We can not fly from our world duties."— Senator Henry Cabot Lodge, 1899
Primary Document · 55th Congress, 3d Session · Senate Doc. No. 62, Part 1

A Treaty of Peace Between the United States and Spain

Signed at the City of Paris, December 10, 1898. Transmitted to the Senate by President McKinley, January 4, 1899.

Spain ceded Cuba, Puerto Rico, and Guam to the United States. The Philippines were sold for $20,000,000.

The Cubans, Filipinos, and Puerto Ricans whose futures were determined at the table in Paris were not present. The transaction was conducted entirely by the empires whose interests it served.

"In 1897, the United States had no overseas territories of significance. By the end of 1898, it had gone from a continental republic to an overseas empire in the span of a single year."

The paperwork of empire — treaties, joint resolutions, court decisions — makes human upheaval look orderly and rational. The disorder, the violence, the human cost are outside the frame of the document. They happen before the signing or after it, in the spaces the formal text does not cover. But they are the real content of what is being signed.

Part Four

The Geography of the New Empire

Empires are geographic projects. The American empire that emerged from 1898 was shaped by the logic of sea power — coaling stations, naval bases, trans-Pacific trade routes, and the economic pull of Asian markets.

Chapter Nine

Why the Islands Mattered

Alfred Thayer Mahan published The Influence of Sea Power Upon History in 1890. The book argued that naval power was the fundamental determinant of national greatness, and that the United States needed a blue-water navy, overseas bases and coaling stations, and force projected into oceanic theaters. Theodore Roosevelt wrote Mahan a congratulatory letter. The Kaiser ordered copies distributed throughout the German fleet. The British Admiralty studied it with interest.

Cuba commanded the Yucatán Channel and the straits between Cuba and Florida. Puerto Rico sat at the eastern Caribbean, commanding the passage between the Atlantic and Caribbean Sea. The Philippines was the gateway to the China market, the forward base from which American naval power could operate in the western Pacific. This argument was made explicitly and without apology. It was an argument for empire, made by men who thought empire was a good thing — provided it was the right empire.

Chapter Ten

Hawaii Was Not an Accident

On January 17, 1893, American businessmen aided by the American minister to Hawaii and backed by the guns of the USS Boston overthrew the Kingdom of Hawaii. Queen Liliuokalani surrendered under protest to avoid bloodshed. The Hawaiian Islands had been, at the start of the nineteenth century, a sovereign Polynesian kingdom with a population of perhaps three hundred thousand people. By the 1890s, the indigenous Hawaiian population had been reduced by disease to perhaps forty thousand.

The formal annexation of 1898 merely made official what had long been true in practice. Hawaii was not an accident. It was a consequence.

Chapter Eleven

Learning to Rule Overseas

The legal machinery for colonial governance was built by the Supreme Court in the Insular Cases, 1901–1904. The question: did the Constitution follow the flag? The Court said no. The territories were "unincorporated" — possessed by the United States but not part of it in the full constitutional sense. The inhabitants were not citizens; they were "nationals," a category invented specifically for this purpose.

Consider what that meant in practice. Isabel González was a Puerto Rican woman who traveled to the United States in 1902 to join her fiancé. She was detained at Ellis Island and ordered deported. Her case went to the Supreme Court. The Court ruled she could not be excluded as an alien — but she was not a citizen. She was a "national": a member of the United States who belonged to it without belonging in it. The Supreme Court used her ordinary legal problem to construct a constitutional architecture for colonial rule. The precedent that was built around her life is still active law.

Part Five

Case Studies in Imperial Contradiction

American imperialism was genuinely contradictory — real idealism and real exploitation intertwined. The contradiction was structural, built into the founding ideology of a republic that could not see itself as an empire.

Chapter Twelve

The Philippines and the Lie of Liberation

"We come, not as invaders or conquerors, but as friends."— General Wesley Merritt, proclamation to the Filipino people, August 1898
Emilio Aguinaldo, c. 1898 Commander of the Philippine revolutionary forces. He declared independence on June 12, 1898 — modeled on the American Declaration of Independence. The United States decided to destroy the government he led. Public Domain · c. 1898

On June 12, 1898, Emilio Aguinaldo declared Philippine independence. His government had a constitution, a congress, a cabinet, a functioning military. It had swept Spanish forces from most of the archipelago outside Manila.

The United States, having promised — through words if not written commitment — to support Philippine independence, now had to decide what to do with an actual, functioning, sovereign Philippine state.

It decided to destroy it.

The Philippine-American War began in February 1899 and did not officially end until 1902 — and continued as guerrilla conflict for years after. The same reconcentration policy for which Spain had been condemned in Cuba was now applied by the United States in the Philippines.

The Philippine-American War · 1899–1913
American combat deaths4,200
American deaths from disease2,800
Filipino civilians killed (estimated)200,000 – 600,000
Official U.S. name for the war"The Philippine Insurrection"
Mark Twain called it"A war of criminal aggression"

The Voice of Dissent — and Why It Lost

The Anti-Imperialist League enrolled over thirty thousand members: Mark Twain, Andrew Carnegie, William James, Jane Addams, Senator George Hoar. Carnegie offered personally to pay the twenty million dollars — simply to make the transaction go away. McKinley declined.

The anti-imperialists lost partly because many of their own arguments were entangled with racism — the fear that incorporating Filipino "savages" into the American republic would degrade it. The racial premise — that Filipinos were not capable of full self-government — was shared by imperialists and anti-imperialists alike. The Filipino voice, which had a very clear answer to the premise, was not part of the conversation.

Chapter Thirteen

Puerto Rico and Permanent Ambiguity

"We were born to be free. What we were given was a cage with better food."— Puerto Rican labor organizer, 1904

Puerto Rico's status within the American political system is, more than a century after 1898, still unresolved. Its residents are American citizens but cannot vote for president and have no voting representation in Congress. This is not an anomaly. It is the design.

When American forces landed in July 1898, they encountered a society that had, just six months earlier, achieved a charter of autonomy from Spain — the most liberal colonial arrangement Spain had ever granted any of its possessions. It was also instantly irrelevant. The arrangement installed by the United States was, in any meaningful sense, less democratic than the autonomy charter it replaced.

Hurricane Maria in 2017 killed an estimated three thousand people — comparable to September 11 — and left the electrical grid destroyed for months. The federal response was inadequate, and the inadequacy was enabled by Puerto Rico's status: close enough to be subject to federal jurisdiction, far enough removed from full constitutional standing that the political pressure to respond adequately was limited. Puerto Rico is not a footnote to the story of 1898. It is one of its clearest expressions.

Chapter Fourteen

Cuba and Incomplete Independence

The Platt Amendment — inserted into the Cuban constitution as a condition of American military withdrawal — gave the United States the right to intervene in Cuba whenever it concluded that Cuban governance had fallen short of American standards. Cuba was independent in name; it was a protectorate in fact. American forces occupied Cuba again from 1906 to 1909, from 1912 to 1913, and from 1917 to 1922.

The Platt Amendment was abrogated in 1934. But by then, American economic dominance was so thorough that formal sovereignty made limited practical difference. American companies owned approximately two-thirds of Cuba's sugar production. The resentments generated accumulated across decades, providing the fuel for the Cuban Revolution of 1959 — a very long-delayed response to 1898.

Part Six

The Pattern Beneath the Event

The individual case studies are also instances of a pattern: imperial power in the process of modernizing, adapting its methods and language to a world where old-fashioned conquest is increasingly difficult to justify.

Chapter Fifteen

When the New Empire Resembles the Old One

Both the Spanish and American imperial systems were organized around the extraction of wealth from the periphery to the center. Both maintained order through a combination of violence and co-optation. Both justified themselves through ideological frameworks that presented domination as service. The words were different; the logic was the same.

Apolinario Mabini — Aguinaldo's prime minister, paralyzed from the waist down — had welcomed American intervention against Spain and turned against American annexation with the specific disappointment of a man who had been deceived by people he trusted. He understood that a democracy could not extend imperial rule without corrupting both the democracy and the subject people. The colonial system did not operate in the dark. It operated in full daylight, against the articulate and documented objections of the people it governed. The schoolroom does not ask the students whether the curriculum is correct.

Chapter Sixteen

The People Who Paid for Transition

"History belongs to those who write it. But it happened to us."— Unattributed; a sentiment expressed across many testimonies

His name was Ramón. He was nineteen. From a village near Zamora in Castile, he had never been more than thirty miles from home when the colonial lottery placed him in the Cuban draft. The families with money paid substitutes. Ramón was a substitute. He had been paid sixty pesetas. He arrived in Havana in March. He was dead by June. Yellow fever. He never fired his rifle in combat.

He does not appear in the Treaty of Paris. He appears here because he is the thing the word "war" most consistently obscures: the specific weight of a specific death, in a specific body, belonging to no abstraction at all. Two hundred thousand Spanish soldiers died in Cuba between 1895 and 1898, the vast majority from disease. Their deaths did not appear in the American press as tragedies. They appeared, when they appeared at all, as evidence of Spanish weakness.

Sergeant Pullen's Question

Sergeant Frank Pullen of the Twenty-Fifth Infantry — the Buffalo Soldiers — fought at El Caney. He watched his men take the hill. He watched those contributions systematically minimized when Roosevelt published his memoir. He wrote: Is America any better than Spain? Has she not subjects in her very midst who are murdered daily without a trial?

The question did not receive a published answer. Pullen had fought for a country that would not let most of them vote, in a war whose outcome produced colonial arrangements structured around the same racial hierarchy they lived under at home.

The Woman in the Camp

She is not named in any record. She was moved in the late spring of 1897. Soldiers came to her village and told the population they had seventy-two hours. She had three children — eight, five, and an infant at the breast. She left the chickens. She left the mango tree. She left the grave of her mother.

The ration was four ounces of dried corn per person per day. The infant did not survive the month. She did not know that American newspapers were writing about her. She did not know that her suffering was being translated, in New York, into a political argument about what the United States should do. She did not know that she was the justification. She appeared in a Spanish military medical report as one entry among thousands: female, rural, deceased family member, camp designation 14, province of Santa Clara.

She was the cost. This is always what the cost looks like at the bottom.

Chapter Seventeen

What 1898 Left Behind

The Insular Cases established a category — "unincorporated territory" — that remained active constitutional doctrine into the twenty-first century. When the government argued in 2002 that Guantánamo detainees had no right to habeas corpus because the base was outside American sovereign territory, the briefs cited Downes v. Bidwell. The constitutional law of American empire is a continuous body of doctrine running from the Philippines in 1901 to Guantánamo Bay without a break.

The counter-insurgency doctrines developed in the Philippines were written into Army field manuals still in circulation when American forces arrived in Vietnam sixty years later. The Small Wars Manual of 1940, which drew explicitly on the Philippine experience, was required reading at Quantico when the officers who designed the early Vietnam strategy were trained. The tactics that failed in Mindanao in 1902 were tried again in the Mekong Delta in 1967.

When Lyndon Johnson explained American intervention in Vietnam as a commitment to the self-determination of the South Vietnamese people, he was using a framework whose logical structure had been designed in 1898 to explain why the self-determination of the Filipino people required their occupation. The vocabulary was old. The sincerity with which it was deployed was new each time. That combination — an inherited structure and a genuine conviction — is what makes it so durable and so difficult to dislodge.

Conclusion

The Empire That Denied Its Name

"The greatest trick the devil ever pulled was convincing the world he didn't exist."
— Attributed to Charles Baudelaire; popularized in The Usual Suspects, 1995

There is a paradox at the heart of the American imperial project that began in 1898, and it is the same paradox that has sustained it: the empire is most powerful precisely when it is most successfully denying that it is an empire.

The denial is built into the founding ideology of the republic. The United States cannot conquer, because it stands for liberty. It cannot colonize, because it was itself a colony. When it does these things, it must find language that makes these acts into something else. And that is what 1898 provided: a fully developed vocabulary for making empire look like liberation.

—   —   —

This book began with what you were taught. It ends with what the people on the other side of the teaching were doing while the lesson was being prepared.

Apolinario Mabini, dying in Manila in 1903, wrote that the Americans had come to the Philippines bearing two gifts: the gift of freedom, and the gift of subjection. The Filipino people, he said, had received only the second. José Martí, dying in Cuba in 1895, had written the night before his death that the independence of Cuba was necessary to prevent a single power from dominating the Americas — and died before he could see how precisely his fear would be confirmed. The woman in Camp 14 planted corn the year after the camp closed because there was nothing else to do. Sergeant Pullen asked whether America was any better than Spain and received no answer that satisfied him. Ramón from Zamora died of yellow fever at nineteen and was recorded in a Spanish military ledger as a number.

These are not footnotes to the story of 1898. They are the story. The schoolroom is still open. Puerto Rico is still a possession. The Insular Cases are still on the books. The checks from Guantánamo are still uncashed in a drawer. The template is still in use.

The most enduring empires are the ones that successfully refuse the name. What this book has tried to do is give it back.

1898: When One Empire Died and Another Began

Mariana Hernandez

Images: "School Begins," Louis Dalrymple, Puck Magazine, 1899 (LOC LC-DIG-ppmsca-28668) · José Martí, Key West, 1891 · USS Maine, Havana Harbor, 1898 (NHHC NH 46774) · Cuban reconcentrado survivors, c. 1897–98 · Emilio Aguinaldo, c. 1898 · Treaty of Paris, Senate Doc. No. 62, 1899 · All images public domain.
